mission.title: Install OPA Gatekeeper on Kubernetes
mission.description: OPA Gatekeeper is the recommended way to run Open Policy Agent on Kubernetes. It acts as a customizable admission webhook that enforces policies executed by OPA. Gatekeeper uses constraint templates and constraints to define and enforce policies at admission time.

steps.title: Install Gatekeeper via Helm
steps.description:
Add the Gatekeeper Helm chart repository and install Gatekeeper into the gatekeeper-system namespace:
```bash
helm repo add gatekeeper https://open-policy-agent.github.io/gatekeeper/charts
helm install gatekeeper/gatekeeper --name-template=gatekeeper --namespace gatekeeper-system --create-namespace
```
Alternatively, install via kubectl apply:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-policy-agent/gatekeeper/v3.21.1/deploy/gatekeeper.yaml
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Gatekeeper controller and audit pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n gatekeeper-system
```
You should see gatekeeper-controller-manager and gatekeeper-audit pods in Running state.

steps.title: Create a constraint template
steps.description:
Define a constraint template that specifies the policy logic in Rego. For example, to require all namespaces to have a specific label:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-policy-agent/gatekeeper-library/master/library/general/requiredlabels/template.yaml
```
Verify the template was created:
```bash
kubectl get constrainttemplates
```

steps.title: Create a constraint
steps.description:
Apply a constraint that uses the template to enforce the policy. For example, require all namespaces to have an 'owner' label:
```yaml
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sRequiredLabels
metadata:
  name: ns-must-have-owner
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        kinds: ["Namespace"]
  parameters:
    labels:
      - key: owner
```
Apply with `kubectl apply -f constraint.yaml` and verify:
```bash
kubectl get constraints
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have Gatekeeper pods running in the gatekeeper-system namespace. Constraint templates and constraints define your policies, which are enforced at admission time. Test by creating a resource that violates a constraint — it should be rejected.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add gatekeeper https://open-policy-agent.github.io/gatekeeper/charts
helm install gatekeeper/gatekeeper --name-template=gatekeeper --namespace gatekeeper-system --create-namespace

kubectl get constrainttemplates
kubectl get constraints

uninstall.title: Remove Gatekeeper via Helm
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Gatekeeper Helm release. Note: Helm v3 does not clean up CRDs automatically.
```bash
helm uninstall gatekeeper -n gatekeeper-system
kubectl delete crd -l gatekeeper.sh/system=yes
kubectl delete ns gatekeeper-system
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Gatekeeper
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repo and upgrade:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade gatekeeper gatekeeper/gatekeeper -n gatekeeper-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Webhook not enforcing policies
troubleshooting.description:
Check that the validating webhook is registered:
```bash
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations | grep gatekeeper
```
If missing, the Gatekeeper controller may not be running.

troubleshooting.title: Constraint violations not blocking
troubleshooting.description:
Verify the constraint is in enforcement mode (not dry-run):
```bash
kubectl get constraints -o yaml | grep enforcementAction
```
Default is 'deny'. If set to 'dryrun' or 'warn', violations are logged but not blocked.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
Check Gatekeeper controller logs:
```bash
kubectl logs -n gatekeeper-system -l control-plane=controller-manager
```

prerequisites.description: Kubernetes 1.16+ with cluster-admin permissions. Helm 3 and kubectl configured to access your cluster.
